fr:mainmatter.html:p: The hardware optimization on GPUs that combines simultaneous memory accesses by threads in a Warp scheduling into a single transaction — when the accessed addresses fall in the same cache line.
Global memory latency on a GPU is 400–800 cycles. Coalescing is the primary way to keep the SM fed:

html:ul.html:li: If all 32 threads in a warp access addresses within one cache line: one memory transaction, full bandwidth.
If addresses span multiple cache lines: the hardware serializes into multiple transactions; bandwidth drops proportionally.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Address permutations within a cache line don't hurt utilization — coalescing is about which lines are touched, not which exact bytes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Coalescing is why memory layout matters so much for GPU code. The standard idiom is structure-of-arrays (SoA) instead of array-of-structures (AoS): threads accessing the same field of consecutive records hit consecutive bytes, which coalesce; threads accessing the same struct don't.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Should you always cache on the GPU?

No. If you're memory-bound and have no Spatial locality, cache is wasted on data you won't reuse. CUDA exposes the option to disable L1 caching for these cases, which lets misaligned loads transfer only the bytes needed instead of full cache lines.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Shared memory on a GPU is a small (per-block) on-chip scratchpad with latency comparable to L1. Software-managed — the programmer explicitly stages data through it, the hardware does not cache for them. Used to:

html:ul.html:li: Avoid non-coalesced global accesses by reorganizing data layouts on the fly.
Reuse loaded data across threads within a block.
Communicate between threads within a block.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Shared memory is divided into banks (typically 32). Each bank can service one access per cycle. If two threads in a Warp scheduling access different addresses in the same bank, the accesses serialize — a bank conflict.
Three behaviors when warp threads access shared memory:

html:ul.html:li: Different banks: full parallelism. No conflict.
Same bank, same word: hardware broadcasts the word to all threads. No conflict.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Change the access pattern so threads hit different banks (often by transposing or by adding padding to break the stride that causes the conflict).

The GPU memory hierarchy is software-managed at L1 (shared memory) and coherent only at L2 — by restricting L1 to local-to-block scope, the architecture sidesteps the cost of a global Cache coherence protocol on a thousand-way-parallel chip.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The hardware optimization on GPUs that combines simultaneous memory accesses by threads in a Warp scheduling into a single transaction — when the accessed addresses fall in the same cache line.
Global memory latency on a GPU is 400–800 cycles. Coalescing is the primary way to keep the SM fed:

html:ul.html:li: If all 32 threads in a warp access addresses within one cache line: one memory transaction, full bandwidth.
If addresses span multiple cache lines: the hardware serializes into multiple transactions; bandwidth drops proportionally.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Address permutations within a cache line don't hurt utilization — coalescing is about which lines are touched, not which exact bytes.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Coalescing is why memory layout matters so much for GPU code. The standard idiom is structure-of-arrays (SoA) instead of array-of-structures (AoS): threads accessing the same field of consecutive records hit consecutive bytes, which coalesce; threads accessing the same struct don't.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Should you always cache on the GPU?

No. If you're memory-bound and have no Spatial locality, cache is wasted on data you won't reuse. CUDA exposes the option to disable L1 caching for these cases, which lets misaligned loads transfer only the bytes needed instead of full cache lines.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A warp is 32 threads on an NVIDIA GPU executing in lockstep — one instruction issued per cycle to all 32. The SM's warp scheduler picks which resident warp to run on each cycle.

The point of the scheduler: hide stalls. When a warp issues a memory operation that misses cache, it can't run for hundreds of cycles. With many resident warps, the scheduler always has a runnable one. Aggregate throughput stays high even though individual-warp latency is awful.

Occupancy is the ratio of active warps to maximum allowed warps per SM. Higher occupancy means more candidates for the scheduler to switch to, which means more latency hiding. Low occupancy means the SM stalls because every resident warp is waiting on the same memory transaction.
For latency-bound workloads (and most GPU code is), maximizing occupancy is the first-order optimization.

When threads in a warp take different branches of a conditional, the SM must serialize: execute one branch with its threads active and the others masked off, then the other. The lanes on the inactive path execute dummy instructions, wasting their compute slots.

html:ul.html:li: Structure code so threads in a warp follow the same control flow when possible.
Warp specialization: have the compiler group threads with the same control-flow pattern into the same warp.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The empirical observation that if a program accesses memory address x, it is likely to access addresses near x soon. Sequential array traversal, struct field access, and instruction fetch all exhibit it.

Caches exploit spatial locality by loading whole cache lines (typically 64 bytes) on every miss, not just the single requested byte. The byte that triggered the load is the critical word; the surrounding bytes come along for free under the assumption you'll want them.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: RAM and caches are organized in cache-line granularity throughout. Bandwidth is sized for whole-line transfers; tags are per-line, not per-byte.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The complement is Temporal locality — repeated access to the same data over time. Temporal locality implies spatial locality (you re-touch nearby bytes when you re-touch the original), but not vice versa.
